narrative:
Markets are sitting comfortably in a business as usual regime, with the stress score at 18 out of 100 confirming a calm backdrop. Equity returns over the past five days have been modestly positive and tightly clustered around zero in z-score terms, meaning there is no unusual momentum in either direction to explain away.

The VIX at 17.7 sits at the 43rd percentile of its ten-year range, which is neither elevated nor suppressed — simply unremarkable. The term structure is in contango, consistent with orderly conditions, and credit is firmly corroborating the picture: high yield spreads at 276 basis points sit at just the 12th percentile of their five-year range, while investment grade at 74 basis points carries a z-score of negative 1.18, meaning both are historically tight and offering no warning signal whatsoever.

Breadth across sectors is clean with no red sectors out of eleven, but the geographic picture is worth a brief note given the magnitude of some moves. Emerging markets broadly have outperformed over the past five days, with EEM up 5.1% and Brazil specifically adding 3.2%, moves that stand out against a relatively flat domestic tape where even the weakest sector, energy, is essentially flat on the week.
